ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for Chinese broccoli as a raw ingredient (FOODON_03301710). No exact entry exists for the stir-fried preparation with special sauce; closest_parent used for the vegetable component.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chinese broccoli (Kailan) — stir-fried vegetable dish; FoodEx2 code A00LC represents leafy vegetables as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for stir-fried kailan with special sauce. A00LC (leafy vegetables) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific prepared vegetable dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried Hong Kong kailan is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a full plate of blanched and stir-fried Chinese broccoli dressed with special sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese broccoli (Kailan), cooked, stir-fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table adapted estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Din Tai Fung's proprietary three-flavour kailan sauce exists in any approved database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked Chinese broccoli (NDB No. 11117 / FDC ID 169967) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for stir-fried leafy vegetables with oyster sauce. Sauce contribution (oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar) estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and proportioned to a typical restaurant sauce volume of ~30 g per 200 g serving. Values represent a best-estimate composite per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy vegetables and mixed vegetable dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried Hong Kong kailan with special sauce. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables (GI range 15–40, typical ~32) and the low-GI contribution of oyster and soy sauce. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (32 × 5.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 3.7, rounded to 3.4 accounting for sauce dilution. This is a low-GI, low-GL dish. Reference: Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving contributes approximately 760 mg sodium, representing about 33% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 3.4 per serving, stir-fried kailan is a suitable vegetable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the sauce adds modest sugar and sodium that should be accounted for in meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, low saturated fat, and beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kailan provides dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g), vitamin C, folate, and potassium, which support cardiovascular health; saturated fat is low at 0.5 g per 100 g, though the sodium from the sauce warrants moderation for those with existing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Hong Kong Kailan with Special Sauce (三味芥兰, San Wei Jie Lan) is a popular Chinese vegetable dish featuring Hong Kong-variety kailan (Chinese broccoli, Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) that is briefly blanched and then stir-fried or dressed with a proprietary three-flavour sauce. At Din Tai Fung in Singapore, the dish is prepared with the restaurant's signature sauce blend, which typically combines oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and a touch of sugar, creating a savoury, slightly sweet, and aromatic coating. The kailan stalks are tender-crisp with a mild bitterness characteristic of the Brassica family, while the sauce adds umami depth and a glossy finish. Nutritionally, this dish is low in calories (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), provides moderate plant-based protein (2.8 g per 100 g), and is rich in dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g), vitamin C (28 mg per 100 g), vitamin A (95 mcg RAE per 100 g), folate (45 mcg DFE per 100 g), and calcium (72 mg per 100 g). The primary clinical consideration is sodium content (approximately 380 mg per 100 g) from the sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (estimated 32), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy, gluten (from oyster sauce), and shellfish allergens. It is a staple side dish in Chinese-Singaporean dining and is commonly ordered alongside rice or noodle mains.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried kailan?
Is Din Tai Fung kailan suitable for diabetics?
What is San Wei Jie Lan?
Is stir-fried Chinese broccoli low GI?
How much sodium is in kailan with oyster sauce?
Is kailan good for heart health?
What allergens are in kailan with special sauce?
What is the difference between kailan and broccoli?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry exists in any approved primary source for Din Tai Fung's proprietary three-flavour kailan preparation. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked Chinese broccoli data combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA estimates for stir-fried leafy vegetables with oyster-based sauce. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy cooked vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base vegetable data is well-supported and the sauce contribution is a standard restaurant formulation with well-characterised components.
notes: 1. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Oils, seasonings and condiments', which is incorrect for a vegetable dish; corrected to 'Vegetables' based on the primary ingredient (kailan). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based stir-fry with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12 (oyster sauce contributes negligible B12 at the quantities used). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as kailan and the sauce components contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the dish is plant-based; oyster sauce contributes negligible cholesterol at typical serving quantities. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked Chinese broccoli data. 7. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Sodium value (380 mg per 100 g) is a key clinical consideration and is estimated from typical oyster sauce and soy sauce contributions in a restaurant-style preparation. 9. Din Tai Fung's exact proprietary sauce recipe is not publicly disclosed; nutrient values should be treated as estimates pending laboratory analysis. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 is used as closest_parent for Chinese broccoli; no exact FoodOn entry for the prepared dish exists. 11. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as closest_parent for leafy vegetables; no exact FoodEx2 code for stir-fried kailan with special sauce exists.
